coredata.dc:title: Efficient decolorization performance of graphene quantum dots modulated nanofiltration membranes with tunable pore size for precise molecular sieving
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Juan
Gong, Ji Lai
Tang, Si Qun
Zhou, Huai Yang
Tang, Liang Xiu
Zhao, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The discharge of wastewater containing dyes not only causes serious environmental pollution but also poses a severe threat to human health. The selective separation of dyes and reuse of inorganic salts are vital in the textile industry. In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was successfully synthesized via the “one-step solvothermal method”, and the N-GQDs composite membranes were innovatively fabricated by constructing a thin selective layer top the modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) matrix membrane via interfacial polymerization process. The pore size of the N-GQDs membranes could be flexibly modulated by regulating the contents of the N-GQDs. The suitable N-GQDs membrane (M4, 0.35 wt% of N-GQDs) possessed over 96 % rejection rate for diverse charged dyes, which was higher than that of the piperazine polyamide (PA3) membrane. Notably, the rejection of the smaller molecular weight of Methylene Blue by the M4 membrane reached ∼98.93 %, which was higher than that reported in most literature, mainly owing to the relatively smaller average pore size and stronger electronegativity of the membrane. Especially compared with our previous work, the prepared M3 and M4 membranes exhibited an enhancement of ∼37.79 % and 39.48 % in rejection of Methylene Blue, ∼ 78 % and 19 % in pure water permeability, respectively. On account of the size sieving and charge effect, the N-GQDs membrane (M4) could maintain high rejection for dyes and exhibited efficient separation for dye/salt with a separation factor of 115 for the Congo Red/sodium chloride mixture after continuous cross-flow filtration for 24 h in the simulated wastewater condition. Furthermore, the N-GQDs membrane also exhibited favorable acid-base resistance and satisfactory anti-fouling performance. Importantly, the N-GQDs membranes presented excellent decolorization efficiency for the practical textile wastewater. These results revealed that the as-prepared N-GQDs membrane possessed great potential for actual textile wastewater advanced treatment and purification. This study also offers a new candidate to rationally manipulate the separation properties of nanofiltration membranes adopting new aqueous monomers.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The discharge of wastewater containing dyes not only causes serious environmental pollution but also poses a severe threat to human health. The selective separation of dyes and reuse of inorganic salts are vital in the textile industry. In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was successfully synthesized via the “one-step solvothermal method”, and the N-GQDs composite membranes were innovatively fabricated by constructing a thin selective layer top the modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) matrix membrane via interfacial polymerization process. The pore size of the N-GQDs membranes could be flexibly modulated by regulating the contents of the N-GQDs. The suitable N-GQDs membrane (M4, 0.35 wt% of N-GQDs) possessed over 96 % rejection rate for diverse charged dyes, which was higher than that of the piperazine polyamide (PA3) membrane. Notably, the rejection of the smaller molecular weight of Methylene Blue by the M4 membrane reached ∼98.93 %, which was higher than that reported in most literature, mainly owing to the relatively smaller average pore size and stronger electronegativity of the membrane. Especially compared with our previous work, the prepared M3 and M4 membranes exhibited an enhancement of ∼37.79 % and 39.48 % in rejection of Methylene Blue, ∼ 78 % and 19 % in pure water permeability, respectively. On account of the size sieving and charge effect, the N-GQDs membrane (M4) could maintain high rejection for dyes and exhibited efficient separation for dye/salt with a separation factor of 115 for the Congo Red/sodium chloride mixture after continuous cross-flow filtration for 24 h in the simulated wastewater condition. Furthermore, the N-GQDs membrane also exhibited favorable acid-base resistance and satisfactory anti-fouling performance. Importantly, the N-GQDs membranes presented excellent decolorization efficiency for the practical textile wastewater. These results revealed that the as-prepared N-GQDs membrane possessed great potential for actual textile wastewater advanced treatment and purification. This study also offers a new candidate to rationally manipulate the separation properties of nanofiltration membranes adopting new aqueous monomers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the rapid development of urbanization and industrialization, the rapid decay of water quality has become an increasingly serious problem. Nearly 10,000 unique dyes and pigments are produced yearly [1]. Dyes are broadly used in the textiles, tannery, cosmetic and paint industries. These dyes are chemically resistant, not biocompatible, and carcinogenic due to the presence of aromatic ring structures, which have deleterious effects on lives. Researchers are currently focusing on developing and exploring the most effective technologies to treat the textile industry's wastewater on account of the chemical, environmental and toxicological features.

Nanofiltration (NF) technology is one of the most efficient processes to remove dyes from wastewater, owing to the properties of effective rejection, energy saving, and accurate selective separation of solute [2–4]. The NF membrane generally consists of a selective layer on the surface and a porous support layer on the substrate. The permeability and selectivity of the NF membranes mostly depend on the active layer's pore size distribution and the charge features of the membrane [5]. Many researchers have tried to improve the performance of the NF membranes by adjusting the surface charge, controlling the pore size, and regulating the thickness of the active layer to break the “trade-off” effect (the constraint between selectivity and permeability) [4–6]. The tailor of pore size distribution of the selective layer has been attempted by adding the porous nanomaterials (such as covalent organic frameworks, metal organic frameworks, and carbon nanotubes [7–10]), introducing an intermediate layer to control the interfacial polymerization reaction, and exploring new reactive monomers [11–13].

It was acknowledged that the introduction of interlayer between the surface selective active layer and the bottom supporting substrate was favorable for improving permeability and/or selectively of the NF membranes [14]. The materials used as interlayer involved nanoparticles, one-dimensional materials, two-dimensional materials, and other interfacial coating layers [8,15–17]. According to the reported literatures, the one-dimensional nanomaterials were more available in raising water permeability and the interfacial coating layers exhibited the most effective in improving separation performance of the membrane [14]. For example, the ultrathin composite polyamide NF membrane supported by single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) showed high water permeability (∼ 40 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) [16]. And the two to five times of water permeability higher than the conventional NF membranes could be achieved when the polydopamine modified SWCNT network used as the interlayer for the composite membranes [18]. The introduction of a poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) interlayer importantly increased the water permeability and antifouling property of the NF membrane [19,20]. These carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and their derivatives used as interlayers were favorable for the formation of thinner selective polyamide layer and relatively larger interfacial areas due to their high aspect ratio, conducing to the enhancement of water permeability for composite membranes. The interfacial coatings used for intermediate layer was beneficial to form defect-free polyamide layer on account of the continuous feature, contributing to the improvement of selectively [14]. Furthermore, the hydrophilic and/or functional interfacial coatings could enhance the uptake of aqueous monomers and control the release rate, deriving the increasement of crosslinking degree and minimization of defects for the selective layer [14,17,21]. It was conducive to the enhancement of the interfacial stability and the formation of thinner and more uniform rejection layer, thereby improving the membrane separation performance in terms of water permeability and selectively [14].

Although introducing nanomaterials improves the water permeability or separation performance of nanofiltration membranes to some extent, it is plagued by incompatibility, poor dispersion, and easy to aggregate [3]. To obtain an ideal and competitive separation performance compared to those fabricated with the conventional monomers (piperazine or m-phenylenediamine), it is urgent to explore and develop a better compatibility and environmentally friendly monomer to manipulate the membrane structure and enhance the separation performance.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, are usually synthesized by “bottom-up” methods [22,23]. The raw materials used to prepare GQDs are widely available and easily accessible, such as citric acid, food wastes, and humic acid [24], endowing GQDs with low toxicity and favorable biocompatibility. The prepared GQDs possess plenty of oxygenated functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl) due to the oxidation or carbonization reactions [25]. The presence of these functional groups enhances the solubility of GQDs and the possibility of expandable functionalization for GQDs. Its chemical structure can be flexibly regulated by the transformation of the raw materials and precursors, which will favor the customization of GQDs to realize the alteration of pore size or surface charge properties of the NF membrane and to regulate the performance of the membranes [24–26]. The modification of GQDs with other functional chemical groups or polymers could endow GQDs with various special features and broaden their application prospects [25]. The functionalized GQDs with more chemical groups was beneficial to constructing a “bridge” between the substrate and the skin layer, and adjusting the structure and properties of the skin active layer, thereby enhancing the integrated stability and performance of the membrane [27,28]. Moreover, the functionalized GQDs used as a monomer could directly react with the organic monomers and conduce to the formation of an active layer with defect-free and thinner, regulating the separation properties of the membranes [28,29].

In most research, GQDs and its functional derivatives have been applied as the aqueous phase monomers, building blocks, or nanofillers into the polyamide selective or intermediate layer of membranes to engineer the properties and structures of the NF membranes [26,30,31]. Because of the graphene-like chemical features of GQDs, the solvent could pass through the membranes introduced with GQDs with less fraction resistance, elevating the permeability of the membranes [32–34]. However, the GQDs-based NF membranes were mainly selective separation of larger dye molecules and were rarely selective removal of relatively small dye molecular contaminants from saline mixed wastewater [23,27,30,32,33,35,36]. In addition, the amino-functional GQDs composite NF membranes exhibited a high rejection rate (∼ 95.18 %) for small molecules of negatively charged Methyl Orange (MO, 327.33 Da) while presenting low rejection (∼ 59.45 %) for positively charged Methylene Blue (MB, 319.85 Da) in our previous work [29]. Moreover, the pathway for synthesis of the amino-functional GQDs materials was more cumbersome. Therefore, the rational design of the GQDs synthesis pathway and targeted functionalization to improve the selective rejection efficiency of different charged organic pollutants with relatively small molecules needs to be well-researched and explored. It will be conducive to improve the selective separation efficacy and application of saline textile wastewaters and better serve sustainable ecological development.

Herein, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was synthesized by a “one-step” hydrothermal approach, and a reasonable design strategy was proposed to construct a unique composite NF membrane with a sandwich structure. The skin selective layer was prepared by N-GQDs as aqueous phase monomers via interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, and the modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with carboxyl groups served as an intermediate layer bridging the surface selection layer and the porous substrate. The physicochemical properties of the N-GQDs membrane were characterized and studied in detail. The optimization design and separation performance of the membranes were performed. Different charged dyes with diverse molecular weights (Methyl Orange (MO), Rhodamine B (RB), Congo Red (CR), Brilliant Green (BG), and Methylene Blue (MB)) and salts (sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), and magnesium chloride (MgCl2)) were adopted to assess the separation performance of the tailored N-GQDs membrane. The anti-fouling and long-term stability of the membrane were also investigated to evaluate the prospective application for actual textile wastewater treatment and recycling.

A commercial polyvinylidene fluoride membrane (PVDF, 0.45 μm) was bought from Xinya Purification Materials Factory (Shanghai, China) and was applied as the porous support substrate. Polyvinyl alcohol 124 (PVA, GR), ethylenediamine (EDA, AR), citric acid (CA, AR), salts (NaCl, Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2; AR), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR), hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR), polyethylene glycol (PEG, CP), and dyes (CR, BG, MB; AR) were provided from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Methyl Orange (MO, AR) and Rhodamine B (RB, AR) were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The n-Hexane (AR) was provided from Hunan Huihong Reagent Co., Ltd. Piperazine (PIP, 99 %) and 1,3,5-Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 98 %) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA, Fraction V, BR) was purchased from GEN-VIEW SCIENTIFIC INC. Lysozyme (LZ, BR) was supplied from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Carboxylic multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were provided by Chengdu Organic Chemicals Co. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs and the modification of substrate membrane
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method [37]. Specifically, 1.68 g of CA was first dispersed in 40 mL of ultrapure water, then 1.6 mL of EDA was added to the above mixture and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined (100 mL) stainless-steel reactor and taken a reaction at 160 °C for 4 h. The synthesis and general structure of N-GQDs are shown schematically in Fig. S1a.

The substrate membrane was modified via two steps: vacuum filtration and grafting. In the first step, 10 mg of MWCNTs was uniformly dispersed in 100 mL of water via sonication. After centrifugation, the homogeneous MWCNTs dispersion was filtrated by vacuum-assisted based on the pristine PVDF substrate membrane. After drying at 60 °C, the obtained membrane was labeled as M substrate membrane. In the second step, the M substrate membrane was immersed in the 1.0 wt% of PVA aqueous solution for 1 h, and then dried at 60 °C for 30 min. The resultant membrane was named the MP substrate membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the N-GQDs NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The composite N-GQDs NF membrane was fabricated by an IP process based on the modified substrate membrane, as shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the MP substrate was fixed in a homemade stainless steel circular device with its selective layer facing up. Then, 30 mL of an N-GQDs aqueous solution was poured into the device and soaked for 30 min at room temperature. After the adsorption procedure of the N-GQDs monomers, the excess solution was poured away and drained by a rubber roller. Subsequently, 50 mL of TMC/n-hexane solution was added to the device and stood for 5 min. The interfacial polymerization crosslinking reaction process between N-GQDs and TMC is roughly shown in Fig. S1b. After draining out the extra solution, the obtained membrane was carefully washed with fresh n-hexane solution for 30 s in order to remove the unreacted monomers. Afterwards, the resultant membrane was put into an oven at 60 °C for 15 min to facilitate and complete the IP process. Finally, the ultimate composite NF membrane was marked as N-GQDs NF membranes. On the basis of the differences of monomers in the IP process, the prepared NF membrane was denoted as M1 ∼ M6 according to the content of N-GQDs varying from 0.10 to 0.65 wt%. The specific monomer's concentrations and the fabrication parameters are presented in Table S1.

The control composite membranes based on the pristine PVDF substrate membrane, M substrate membrane, and PVDF-PVA substrate membrane (noted as P substrate membrane) were also prepared to select the suitable interlayer. The interfacial polymerization process was the same as the preparation of N-GQDs NF membrane with 0.35 wt% of N-GQDs, 0.20 wt% of TMC, and a reaction time of 5 min. The corresponding composite membranes were denoted as the PVDF-(N-GQDs), M-(N-GQDs), and P-(N-GQDs) membranes, respectively.

The morphologies of as-prepared membranes were carried out by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300). The morphology of N-GQDs materials was characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200). The mean roughness of the membrane surface was performed via atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon). A contact angle measurement instrument (Lauda Scientific LSA100) characterized the membrane's static water contact angle (WCA). The membrane surface of charge properties was conducted by a Zeta potential analyzer (Anton Paar surpass). The chemical structures of N-GQDs and the membranes were measured by Fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha), respectively.

The separation performance of the membranes was evaluated by cross-flow flat membrane experimental equipment, as depicted in Fig. S2. The effective filtration area of the membrane device is 9.62 cm2, and the filtration pressure was set at 0.4 MPa (4 bar). The prepared membrane was pre-stabilized with pure water at 4 bar for at least 30 min before the formal filtration testing. Five dyes with different charges and molecular weights (MB, BG, RB, CR, and MO; 10 mg/L) and four typical inorganic salts (Na2SO4, NaCl, MgSO4, and MgCl2; 1.0 g/L) were selected to measure the filtration properties of the prepared membranes. Besides, PEGs with molecular weights of 300 to 1000 Da (10 mg/L) were utilized to calculate the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the membranes. The molecular weight of PEG is considered as the MWCO of the membranes when the rejection rate of PEG reaches 90 %. The concentration of PEG, salts, and dyes in solution were determined by a total organic carbon meter (TOC, Shimadzu), a conductivity meter (Mettler Toledo, SevenEasy), and an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu), respectively. The membrane's water permeability (WP, L m−2 h−1 bar−1), solute rejection (R, %), and selective separation factor (F) of dye to salt can be calculated using the following (1) ∼ (3) equations [6,38], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fouling resistance performance and stability of the membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
Membrane fouling is an inevitable phenomenon during the operation of nanofiltration. It is caused by the physicochemical interaction of biological protein macromolecules, natural organic matter, or colloidal particles with the membrane surface, resulting in the accumulation or blockage of membrane pores and the decay of membrane filtration performance. Good anti-fouling properties are beneficial to prolong the service of the membrane. BSA and LZ (1.0 g/L), two typical bioprotein macromolecule representatives with different charge properties, were selected as the model pollutants to measure the fouling resistance of the membranes. The experiment was carried out with the following five steps. (1) The fabricated membrane was pre-compacted with ultrapure water for 30 min at 4 bar to reach a steady state, and then the pure water permeance (P
w0 , L m−2 h−1 bar−1) was recorded at regular intervals. (2) The BSA or LZ was used as the feed solution, and the permeance (P
p , L m−2 h−1 bar−1) was monitored. (3) The contaminated membrane was washed with flowing pure water for 30 min in the equipment. (4) The pure water was used as the feed solution again to detect the permeance (P
w1 , L m−2 h−1 bar−1), and the first cycle of the fouling resistance test was complete. (5) Steps (2) to (4) were repeated to accomplish the second pollutant resistance measurement. The water permeance recovery rate (FRR, %), total permeance decay rate (FDR
t , %), reversible permeance decay rate (FDR
r , %), and irreversible permeance decay rate (FDR
ir , %) in each experiment cycle were calculated with the following (4) ∼ (7) equations, respectively.
(4)
FRR =

The operation stability of the fabricated optimal N-GQDs membrane was detected using CR/NaCl (10 / 1000 mg/L) mixture solution as the representative pollutants. The continuous filtration test was employed for 24 h at environment temperature. In order to minimize the influence of the membrane contamination and the concentration polarization, a simple water flow rinse was performed for 30 min after every 8 h of the filtration experiment. Additionally, in order to test the potential of composite membranes in practical applications, the rejection performance of the membranes for the high concentration of CR dye (100, 500, and 1000 mg/L) and the decolorization performance for the actual wastewater were conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of materials and composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of the M and MP substrate membrane are shown in Fig. S3. Carbon nanotube materials with tubular structure can be clearly observed on the surface of the M substrate membrane (Fig. S3a). On the surface of the MP substrate membrane, a thin layer of polymer is attached, and the carbon nanotube materials can still be fuzzy observed (Fig. S3b). The FTIR spectra of the M substrate membrane and MP substrate membrane are shown in Fig. S4. The peaks at 1708 and 1259 cm−1 are attributed to the CO and CO bonds in carboxylic groups [39], respectively, in the FTIR spectrum of the M substrate membrane. In the spectrum of the MP substrate membrane, the characteristic broad peak at around 3222 cm−1 is attributed to the -OH groups. The peak at 2897 cm−1 is ascribed to CH vibration. The peak appearing at around 1058 cm−1 is due to the CO stretching vibration [40] (Fig. S4). The SEM and FTIR results indicated that the PVA polymer adhered successfully to the surface of the M substrate membrane.

The FTIR spectra of N-GQDs materials and the composite membranes are presented in Fig. 2 . In the spectrum of N-GQDs material (Fig. 2a), the characteristic peaks at 3348, 3287 cm−1 are assigned to NH bond (−NH2). The OH stretching vibration is overlapped at around 3348 cm−1. The peaks at 1645 and 1544 cm−1 belong to the CO and NH bonds [41,42], respectively. It indicated that the citric acid was reacted with ethylenediamine and induced the successful synthesis of N-GQDs material. The peaks at 1368 and 1080 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of CN and CO bonds, respectively. The 1744 and 1433 cm−1 peaks are related to the stretching vibration of CO and bending vibration of -OH of the carboxyl group [43], respectively. The size of the aggregates of N-GQDs in solution was about 17.11 ± 2.92 nm according to the SEM images (Fig. S5a and b). The size of the N-GQDs was about 3.73 ± 1.06 nm according to the TEM images (Fig. S5c). A small number of N-GQDs can reach a size of ∼9 nm (Fig. S5d). The morphology of the N-GQDs was nearly circular with distinct lattice stripes (Fig. S5c and d).

For the FTIR spectra of the M3 and M4 membranes (Fig. 2b), the peaks appeared at almost the same position, and the peak intensity of M4 was stronger than that of the M3 membrane. The peak at 3237 cm−1 is associated with the overlap of vibration of NH and OH bonds. The peaks at 1630 and 1534 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of the typical amide groups. The peak that appeared at 1715 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO bond [42]. It may be the carboxyl groups hydrolyzed from the unreacted acyl chloride groups in TMC or the ester groups formed by the reaction of the hydroxyl groups with the acyl chloride groups [31,42]. These results confirmed that the interfacial polymerization reaction had occurred successfully between the N-GQDs and TMC.

To observe the surface morphology and cross-section structure of the M3 and M4 membrane, SEM was performed and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . The surface morphology of the M3 membrane presented a polyamide nodule granular layer (Fig. 3a). The cross-section SEM image exhibit the thickness of the layer was about 51.14 nm (Fig. 3c). With the increasing concentration of N-GQDs, the M4 membrane surface became denser and smoother (Fig. 3b and f). The thickness of the active layer of the M4 membrane was increased. However, the average roughness was reduced compared to the M3 membrane (Fig. 3). The average roughness of the M4 membrane surface was around 63.6 nm (Fig. 3f). The lower roughness is beneficial to enhance the property of anti-fouling and extend the service life of the membrane.

The surface chemical functional groups of the N-GQDs materials, M3 and M4 membranes were further investigated through the XPS survey spectra. The C1s spectrum of the N-GQDs materials could be divided into C-C/C-H/C=C (284.49 eV), C-N/C-O (285.83 eV), and O-C=O/N-C=O (287.50 eV), respectively (Fig. S6b). In the spectrum of N1s, the peaks at 398.69, 399.22, and 400.55 eV corresponded to the NH2, N-C=O, and NH groups, respectively (Fig. S6c). For the O1s spectrum, the peaks at 530.40, 531.04, 532.21, and 537.96 eV were attributed to O=C-N, O=C-O, CO, and aromatic-O-H, respectively (Fig. S6d).

For the XPS spectra of the membranes, the C1s spectrum of the M3 membrane was divided into C-C/C-H/C=C (284.84 eV), C-N/C-O (286.22 eV), and O-C=O/N-C=O (287.85 eV), respectively (Fig. 4a) [27]. In the spectrum of N1s, the peaks at 398.92, 399.60, and 401.35 eV corresponded to the NH2, N-C=O, and NH groups, respectively (Fig. 4b) [31]. For the O1s spectrum, the peaks at 531.06, 532.05, 533.15, and 537.81 eV were attributed to O=C-N, O=C-O, CO, and aromatic-O-H, respectively (Fig. 4c) [44,45]. Compared with the results of the XPS analysis of N-GQDs materials, the content of NH and -NH2 functional groups in the N1s spectrum of the membranes exhibited a significant decrease and a great increase of N-C=O groups in the N1s and O1s spectra (Table S2). These results demonstrate that the polyamide layer was generated, which was consistent with the analysis of FTIR and SEM (Fig. 2 and Fig. 3). The XPS features of the M4 membrane were similar to those of the M3 membrane, except that there was a change in the content of the functional groups (Fig. S7 and Fig. 4). Compared to the M3 membrane, N-C=O functional groups increased, while the NH2 group decreased in the XPS N1s high-resolution spectra of the M4 membrane (Fig. 4e and Table S2). Moreover, the O/N element content of the M4 membrane decreased to 1.34, and the corresponding cross-linking degree increased to 0.57 (Table S3), forming a denser selective layer. Similar results could also be obtained and observed from the surface morphology of SEM (Fig. 3). All these results confirmed the successful fabrication of N-GQDs NF (M3 and M4) membrane and the increase of the cross-linking degree with increasing N-GQDs concentration.

The separation performances of MB by composite membranes prepared with different interlayers were shown in Fig. S8. The P-(N-GQDs) membrane modified by the PVA interlayer had the largest pure water permeability of 17.40 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 but poor MB rejection (62.62 %). The M-(N-GQDs) membrane modified by the MWCNTs interlayer obtained relatively high rejection (95.45 %) for MB, but the pure water permeability was low at 3.54 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. The pure PVDF membrane loaded with the N-GQDs selective layer had a pure water permeability of 9.58 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with a medium rejection rate of MB (78.82 %). The MP-(N-GQDs) membrane prepared with MWCNTs and PVA composite interlayer (MP interlayer) obtained the highest rejection rate of MB (97.31 %), and the pure water permeability was about 5.19 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. Combining the results of the membrane's rejection rate of MB and the water permeability, it suggested that the appropriate modification of the PVDF substrate membrane was the MP interlayer to load N-GQDs materials and prepare further composite membranes.

The concentration of TMC was fixed at 0.20 wt% and the reaction time of IP was 5 min. The effect of the N-GQDs concentration (0.10–0.65 wt%) on the separation performance of the composite membranes toward MB was performed, and the results were shown in Fig. 5a. The rejection rate of MB by the membranes improved (90.90–99.46 %) with the increased concentration of N-GQDs. Inversely, the permeability of the membranes declined (31.92–1.94 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) as the contents of N-GQDs increased. This trend is linked to the usual “trade-off” effect. Specifically, when the concentration of N-GQDs was increased from 0.20 to 0.35 wt%, the rejection of MB by the membranes (M2, M3, and M4) was >95 %, but the pure water permeability significantly decreased from 30.29 to 5.19 L m−2 h−1 bar−1.

In order to better select the optimal composite nanofiltration membranes, the separation performances of MO and RB dye molecules with different electric charges and molecular weights were tested using M2, M3, and M4 membranes. The results are shown in Fig. 5b, where the rejection of both MO and RB by the M2 membrane was below 80 %. As the concentration of N-GQDs increased, the rejection of MO and RB by the M3 and M4 membranes also increased. However, the rejection of the M3 membrane for MO and RB was still not very high, which were 87.58 % and 60.22 %, respectively. With the further increase of the concentration of N-GQDs, the rejection of MO and RB by the M4 membrane increased considerably, reaching 95.11 % and 91.10 %, respectively. This may be due to the fact that at lower concentrations of N-GQDs, it is difficult for the aqueous monomers with low concentrations to form a complete active layer during the IP process, resulting in a low rejection of the target pollutants. With the increase of aqueous phase monomer concentration, the active layer gradually tends to form a dense and defect-free structure, which is conducive to the rejection of the target pollutants by the composite membranes but also leads to the reduction of the membrane's permeability. It is consistent with the experimental results in Fig. 5.

According to the experimental results of the effect of N-GQDs concentration on the separation performance of composite membranes, M3 and M4 membranes were selected to carry out experimental studies on the separation performance of MB, MO and RB, respectively. The concentration of TMC varied from 0.05 to 0.30 wt%, and the IP reaction time was fixed at 5 min. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 6 .

The rejection of MB by the M3 membranes synthesized at different TMC concentrations was >94 %. The pure water permeability of the membranes increased with the TMC concentration increasing (Fig. 6a). Especially when the TMC concentration exceeded 0.15 wt% and reached 0.20 wt%, the pure water permeability of the M3 membrane increased significantly to 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. However, the rejection of MB decreased by 1.6 % compared with that at 0.15 wt%. It might be due to the fact that the degree of interfacial polymerization was incomplete when the concentration of TMC was small, resulting in the presence of many defects in the formed active selective layer. MB dye molecules could enter into the modified MWCNTs intermediate layer through the defects, leading to the adsorption for MB. The adsorption of MB would occupy part of the water transport channel and block the membrane pores, resulting in relatively low water permeability. With the increase of TMC concentration, adequate TMC participated in the interfacial polymerization process and gradually tended to form a defect-free selective layer. In this case, the main separation of MB was the rejection effect by the membrane surface, so the water permeability increased compared with that at low concentrations. Therefore, the concentration of TMC (0.15 wt%) at the best rejection rate for MB (98.15 %) was chosen as the concentration of organic phase monomer for the subsequent preparation of M3 membranes.

As shown in Fig. 6b, the rejection of MO and RB by the M4 membrane first increased and then slightly decreased with increasing TMC concentration. On the contrary, the water permeability of the membrane decreased first and then increased. At a TMC concentration of 0.15 wt%, the M4 membrane showed the best rejection of MO at 96.70 %, but the membrane had the lowest pure water permeability (Fig. 6b and Fig. S9). When the concentration of TMC is 0.10 wt%, the rejection of RB by the M4 membrane was best (96.08 %), and the pure water permeability of the membrane was about 3.97 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. Combining the results of the rejection rate and water permeability of the M4 membranes for MO and RB, a TMC concentration of 0.10 wt% was selected as the concentration of the organic phase monomer for the subsequent preparation of M4 membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction time of the interfacial polymerization process
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of IP reaction time on the separation performance of M3 and M4 membranes were investigated using MB and MO as the target contaminants, respectively. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be observed that the rejection of MB and MO by the M3 and M4 membranes gradually increased, and the water permeability gradually decreased with the extension of IP reaction time. This is related to the thickness and integrity of the selective layer formed by the composite membrane during the IP process. When the IP reaction time is too short, the contact time of the two-phase monomer is inadequate. Thus, the formed selective layer is very thin and probably incomplete, generating some defects and resulting in a relatively low rejection of the target pollutants. When the IP reaction time is prolonged, the diffusion process between the two-phase monomers is extended, and the formed selective layer gradually becomes thicker and tends to be defectless. The resultant composite membrane presented an increased resistance to the transport of water molecules, which led to a decrease in the water permeability. At the same time, the selective separation performance for the target pollutants will be improved by the dense and defect-free selective layer.

As shown in Fig. 7a, when the IP reaction time was increased from 0.5 to 1 min, the rejection of MB by the M3 membrane increased from 92.76 % to 97.24 %, and the pure water permeability of the membrane slightly decreased from 10.46 to 9.75 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. With the further increase of the IP reaction time to 7 min, the rejection rate of MB by the M3 membrane was 98.49 %, and its pure water permeability decreased to 5.42 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. The separation performance of the M4 membrane for MO presented a similar trend (Fig. 7b). Based on the experimental results of the separation performance of the M3 and M4 membranes for MB and MO, the IP reaction time for the preparation of composite membranes was chosen to be 1 min in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the separation performance of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the separation performance of the membranes for different dyes and inorganic salts and to better understand the separation mechanisms, the separation experiments of the M3 and M4 membranes for five dyes (CR, MO, RB, BG, and MB) and four salts (NaCl, Na2SO4, MgCl2, and MgSO4) were carried out. The traditional polyamide NF membrane prepared by PIP aqueous phase monomer with a concentration of 0.30 wt% was used as the control (TMC concentration was 0.15 wt%, the IP reaction time was 1 min, and the prepared membrane was denoted as the PA3 membrane).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation properties of the membranes for organic dyes and inorganic salts
ce:section.ce:para: The separation results of the M3, M4, and PA3 membranes for dyes and salts were exhibited in Fig. 8 . The M3 membrane showed the highest water permeability for filtration of dye molecules (8.74–9.49 L m−2 h−1 bar−1). The M4 and PA3 membranes displayed comparable water permeability with 5.06–6.47 and 4.87–6.19 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively (Fig. 8a). The pure water permeability of the M3 membrane was about 1.6 times that of the PA3 membrane. In terms of rejection performance, the M4 membrane presented the best comprehensive results. The rejections for MO, CR, MB, and BG dyes were all >96 % (96.66 %, 99.66 %, 98.93 % and 98.48 %, respectively) by the M4 membrane (Fig. 8b). The rejection of dyes by the M3 membrane was slightly lower than that of the M4 membrane, and the rejection of MO, CR, MB and BG dyes was over 95 %. The rejection rate of MO, CR, and MB by the PA3 membrane was comparable to the results of the M3 membrane, but the rejection of BG (91.21 %) was lower than that of the M3 membrane (96.76 %), and the rejection of RB (77. 71 %) was lower than that of the M4 membrane (79.29 %) (Fig. 8b).

Fig. 8c showed the different water permeability of the composite membranes for four salts. It was related to the charge properties of the membrane surface, its physicochemical features and the nature of the salt ions. The water permeability of the M3 membrane was higher than that of the M4 membrane, with the same trend for different salts (MgCl2 > NaCl > MgSO4 > Na2SO4 (Fig. 8c)). The water permeabilities of the PA3 membranes were generally comparable to that of the M4 membrane (except for NaCl), with the order of NaCl > MgCl2 > Na2SO4 > MgCl2. The rejections of the M4 membrane for salts were higher than that of the M3 membrane. Moreover, the rejection rates of the M4 membrane for Na2SO4 (85.21 %), NaCl (46.20 %), and MgCl2 (66.60 %) were also higher than that of the PA3 membrane (Fig. 8d). The rejection of the PA3 membrane for MgSO4 was the highest at 82.46 %, and the rejection for the Na2SO4 and NaCl presented roughly the same results as that of the M3 membrane (Fig. 8d). The water permeability of the M3 membrane was higher than that of the PA3 membrane during the filtration of MgSO4 and MgCl2 solutions (Fig. 8c). The rejection rate of MgSO4 and MgCl2 by the M3 membrane (42.98 % and 34.30 %) was lower than that of the PA3 membrane (82.46 % and 40.14 %) (Fig. 8d). Considering the combination results of the rejection properties of dyes, the comparable dye rejection and lower salt rejection of the M3 membrane were more conducive to potential separation dye/salt mixture applications compared to the PA3 membrane.

The comparison of the separation performance for organic dyes and inorganic salt between the M4 membrane prepared with 0.15 wt% of TMC (denoted as M4–0.15 membrane) and the PA3 membrane was also performed. As shown in Fig. S10, the M4–0.15 membrane also exhibited high rejection (> 96 %) for charged dyes (MB: 99.88 %; MO: 96.67 %; CR: 99.53 %; and BG: 98.25 %). The rejection of RB by the M4–0.15 membrane was moderate (∼ 80.09 %) (Fig. S10a). The M4–0.15 membrane displayed a slightly higher rejection rate of most organic dyes than the PA3 membrane, with a mildly lower water permeability than the PA3 membrane (Fig. S10a and Fig. 8). The rejection rates of the M4–0.15 membrane for Na2SO4 and MgCl2 were about 82.28 % and 49.08 % (Fig. S10b), respectively, which were slightly higher than those of the PA3 membrane (∼ 76.64 % for Na2SO4 and ∼ 40.14 % for MgCl2). The rejection rates of MgSO4 (∼ 66.23 %) and NaCl (∼ 30.68 %) were lower than those of the PA3 membrane, and the water permeabilities of the M4–0.15 membrane were slightly lower than those of the PA3 membrane when filtration of inorganic salt (Fig. S10b and Fig. 8). It might be related to the MWCO, pore size distribution, and structure properties of the membranes.

As shown in Fig. S11, the MWCO of the N-GQDs membranes decreased from ∼774 to 426 Da with the increase of N-GQDs contents varying from 0.10 to 0.65 wt%, and the corresponding average effective pore size decreased from ∼0.88 to 0.48 nm. As the concentration of N-GQDs increased, the particle aggregation phenomenon was gradually observed on the surface of the N-GQDs membrane, especially for the M5 and M6 membranes with higher concentrations (Fig. S12). The thickness of the active layer of N-GQDs membranes also increased gradually from ∼47.06 to 129.41 nm (Fig. S12). The surface of the M4–0.15 membrane displayed a relatively flat appearance, and the thickness of the active layer was about 82.35 nm (Fig. S12e, f), which was lower than that of the PA3 membrane (∼ 113.64 nm, Fig. S13b). The MWCO of the M4–0.15 membrane was about 481 Da (Fig. S11), which was smaller than the PA3 membrane (∼ 534 Da, Fig. 9 ). The average effective pore size of the M4–0.15 membrane (∼ 0.58 nm) was slightly less than that of the PA3 membrane (∼ 0.66 nm). These combined results were concerned with the different separation performances for organic dyes and inorganic salts.

The comprehensive results of the separation performance of the composite membranes showed that the M4 membrane presented better rejection performance for dye molecules and most of the salts than the PA3 membrane with comparable water permeabilities. Besides, the M3 membrane exhibited higher water permeability than the PA3 membrane, with a similar rejection. Under the same preparation conditions, the composite membranes prepared with N-GQDs showed better comprehensive separation properties than the polyamide NF membranes prepared by the traditional monomer of PIP. Moreover, the thickness of the selective layer and the pore size could be conveniently manipulated by regulating the contents of N-GQDs, conducing to satisfy different separation demands.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation mechanisms of the N-GQDs membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The rejection sequence of different dyes by the M3 and M4 membranes is CR > MB > BG > MO > RB. The rejection order of the PA3 membrane for dyes is CR > MB > MO > BG > RB. The separation mechanisms of nanofiltration membranes are mainly charged effects and sieving effects [5,46,47]. For better exploring the rejection mechanisms of the N-GQDs membranes (M3 and M4 membrane) and PA3 membrane for dyes or salts, the Zeta potential and molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the membranes were performed. As shown in Fig. S14, the ζ-potential of the M3, M4, and PA3 membranes is about −47.44, −43.33 and − 48.70 mV at pH ≈ 7, respectively, indicating that the surfaces of the three composite membranes are negatively charged under neutral conditions. Additionally, CR and MO are negatively charged dyes, MB and BG are positively charged dyes, and RB presents a zwitterionic form in a neutral solution [48,49]. Therefore, the rejection of M3 and M4 composite membranes was >95 % for CR, MO, MB, and BG due to the electrostatic interactions between the surface of the N-GQDs membranes and the charged dye molecules. Whereas, the rejection of electroneutral RB was <80 % due to the lack of significant electrostatic interactions. It illustrated that the electrostatic interaction between dye molecules and membrane surface was primary and crucial for the rejection of dyes.

Besides, the size sieving effect also plays an essential role for the rejection of dyes with the same charge type owing to the divergent rejection rate, such as CR and MO (anionic), MB and BG (cationic). The molecular weight of CR (696 Da) was greater than MO (327 Da), agreeing with the rejection sequence of CR and MO by the membranes. For the cationic dyes of MB and BG, the molecular weight of MB (319 Da) was smaller than BG (482 Da), while the rejection rates of MB and BG by the M3 and M4 membranes were pretty close. In addition, although the molecular weight of MO (327 Da) was adjacency to MB (319 Da), the membranes presented a higher rejection rate to MB (Fig. 8b). Moreover, the MWCO of the M4 and M3 membranes is about 469 Da and 567 Da (Fig. 9a), respectively, which was higher than the molecular weight of MO and MB. It illustrated that the rejection rate was not solely affected by the molecular weight of the solute but was also influenced by the molecular dimension and structural shape of dyes [50].

The pore radius distribution of the membranes is displayed in Fig. 9b. The average pore diameter of the M4 membrane (∼ 0.46 nm) is smaller than the M3 membrane (∼ 0.60 nm) and the PA3 membrane (∼ 0.66 nm). Besides, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the pore radius distribution curve of the M4 membrane is the smallest (∼ 0.257 nm), which indicates that the pore size distribution of the M4 membrane is more uniform than the M3 and PA3 membranes. In terms of the size sieving effect mechanism, the M4 membrane will obtain the best rejection for contaminants. The experimental results are precisely in line with this (Fig. 8b). According to the results in Fig. 8, the membranes exhibited the highest rejection rate of CR (> 99.5 %) on account of the largest molecular size of CR (2.56 × 0.73× 1.13 nm) and the three-dimensional size of CR was larger than the average effective pore size of the membranes [51] (Fig. 8b). The molecular size of MO (1.31 × 0.70 × 0.50 nm) was slightly smaller than MB (1.53 × 0.65 × 0.28 nm) [52], partially leading to the relatively low rejection rate than MB. Furthermore, the short molecular axial dimension of MO with chain structure was less resistant to penetrate through the selective layer of the membrane [50]. Nevertheless, a relatively high rejection of the membranes for MO (> 95 %) was achieved due to the sufficiently strong electrostatic repulsion between the anionic MO and the negatively charged membranes. Therefore, the rejection of dyes by the N-GQDs membrane is mainly a synergetic effect of electrostatic interaction and size sieving effect, in which the electrostatic interaction between dyes and the membranes is predominant.

With regard to the salts, the M3 membrane expressed the rejection order as Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > NaCl > MgCl2 (Fig. 8d), conforming to the most typical negatively charged NF membrane [53]. The M4 membrane also displayed the highest rejection for Na2SO4 and the lowest rejection for NaCl. The divalent anions exhibited higher rejection than the monovalent anions [54]. The rejection mechanisms could be explained by the following reasons. On the one hand, the negatively charged surface of the membrane would repulse anions and attract cations, leading to a higher rejection of bivalent anions (SO4 2−) than the bivalent cations (Mg2+) and monovalent anions (Cl−). On the other hand, the hydrated ionic radius of SO4 2− is about 0.38 nm, which is slightly higher than the Cl− (0.33 nm) and Na+ (0.36 nm) while smaller than the Mg2+ (0.43 nm) [6]. Therefore, according to the combination of the steric hindrance (size exclusion) and Donnan effect (charge effect), the highest rejection rates of Na2SO4 by the N-GQDs membranes (M3 and M4 membrane) were achieved within four kinds of salt solution. Besides, the rejection rate of Na2SO4 was enhanced by 8.46 % and 8.58 % for the M4 membrane with the smaller average pore size compared to the M3 and PA3 membranes, respectively. It indicates that the decrease in the average pore size of the composite membrane is conducive to improving the rejection of Na2SO4. Moreover, at pH ≈ 7, the membrane surface Zeta potential of the M3 membrane (−47.44 mV) was slightly higher than that of the M4 membrane (−43.33 mV), which indicated that the steric hindrance effect played a crucial role in the salt rejection. Hence, the rejection mechanism of the N-GQDs composite membranes for salt is mainly relevant to the steric hindrance effect and Donnan effect (Fig. 10 ).

We compared the separation performance of dyes and salts by diversiform membranes reported in the literature [29,50,51,55–58] (Table 1 ). The prepared N-GQDs membrane exhibits a >95 % rejection rate for all tested charged dyes with a comparable water permeability, higher than the majority of other membranes, especially for the smaller organic dyes of MO and MB. For example, an increase of ∼39.48 % (from 59.45 % to 98.93 %) for the MB rejection and an elevation of ∼19 % for pure water permeability were achieved by the M4 membrane when compared to that of the M-TA-NGQDs membrane [29]. It may be due to the alteration of the raw materials and precursors for the synthesis of graphene carbon quantum dots, so that a smaller average effective pore size, more uniform distribution, and a stronger electronegativity of the selective layer formed between the synthesized N-GQDs and TMC. In addition, compared to the NGQDs membranes reported in our previous work, the rejection of MO by the M3 membrane was improved by about 7.61 % while retaining a considerable pure water permeability [29]. The pure water permeability of the M3 membrane was enhanced ∼78 % compared with that of the M-TA-NGQDs membrane [29]. Furthermore, the M3 membrane is also better than the conventional PA3 membrane's rejection performance synthesized under the same conditions. Moreover, the N-GQDs composite membrane can flexibly regulate and design the pore size of the membrane by adjusting the contents of N-GQDs so as to separate the target pollutants directionally. Therefore, the N-GQDs composite membranes synthesized in this experiment have the potential to separate industrial textile wastewater by step purification.

The separation performance of the different CR concentrations by the M3, M4, and PA3 membranes was presented in Fig. S15. With the increase of concentration from 100 to 1000 mg/L, the water permeability of the membranes decreased gradually. It might be due to the concentration polarization and fouling on the membrane surface being enhanced with the concentration increasing, resulting in a reduction of water permeability [59]. However, the rejection rates of CR were all above 99 % and changed inconspicuously. It is mainly due to the fact that CR is a negatively charged dye with a larger molecular size, and the conjunctive effects of strong electrostatic repulsion and the steric hindrance effect contributed to the maintenance of high rejection of CR by the membranes. The rejection of CR by the membranes was slightly increased with concentrations varying from 100 to 500 mg/L. It might be attributed to the aggregation of dye molecules and the formation of dye clusters [46,60]. These results exhibited that the M3 and M4 membranes possessed good separation performance for high CR concentrations, indicating the potential for practical applications.

Membrane fouling is a severe challenge for separation membranes. Membrane contamination will lead to an inevitable decline in membrane permeability and separation efficiency during long-term operation. Good fouling resistance will extend the service life of the membrane and reduce its maintenance cost. The results of the fouling resistance experiments are shown in Fig. 11 and Table 2 . After two cycles of contamination cycling, the water permeability of the PA3 membrane significantly decreased compared to the initial pure water permeability (BSA: ∼29.92 %; LZ: ∼20.80 %). The water permeability of the M3 membrane decreased by about 28.56 % and 30.49 % when passing through the BSA and LZ stock solutions, respectively, compared to that of the original pure water permeability (Fig. 11a). The M4 membrane showed a decrease in water permeability of about 24.37 % compared with the original pure water permeability when filtering the BSA solution. The trend of decrease in water permeability was more moderate at about 10.89 % when filtering the LZ solution (Fig. 11a). Moreover, after two pollution cycles and simple hydraulic flushing, the M4 membrane showed the highest average water permeance recovery rate (BSA: 97.80 ± 0.47 %; LZ: 97.62 ± 0.75 %) and the lowest irreversible permeance attenuation rate (BSA: 2.20 ± 0.47 %; LZ: 2.38 ± 0.75 %) (Fig. 11b and c). It indicates that the contamination of BSA and LZ on the surface of the M4 membrane is primarily dominated by physical deposition, and >97 % of the permeability can be effectively recovered by simple hydraulic flushing, implying that the M4 membrane has superior fouling resistance comprehensively. The satisfactory antifouling property of the M4 membrane is mainly related to the smaller average pore size (∼ 0.46 nm), lower roughness surface (∼ 63.6 nm, Fig. 3 and Fig. S13) and strong hydrophilic surface (WCA: 33.6 ± 2.07°, Fig. S16) of the M4 membrane, which forms a hydration layer that effectively mitigates the adsorption and deposition of hydrophobic organic matters on the membrane surface. The combination of these factors and the strong electronegativity characteristics of the membrane surface endowed the M4 membrane with excellent fouling resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity, stability, and acid-alkaline resistance of the N-GQDs membranes, experiments of the changes in permeability and rejection performance of CR/NaCl mixed solution by the M3, M4 and PA3 membranes were conducted under neutral, acidic (pH ≈ 3), alkaline (pH ≈ 12) and simulated effluent (pH ≈ 7, The specific components of the configuration are shown in Table S4 of the Supplementary Material.) ambient conditions for 24 h continuous operation, respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 12 . The average CR rejections of M3 and PA3 membranes were maintained above 99 % with only minor fluctuations under neutral, alkaline and simulated effluent conditions (Fig. 12a, c, d). The rejection of CR decreased slightly under acidic conditions, with average values of about 98.96 ± 0.30 % (M3) and 98.92 ± 0.44 % (PA3), respectively (Fig. 12b). It may be mainly related to the positive charge of the membrane surface under acidic conditions (pH ≈ 3) (Fig. S14). The M4 membrane was able to maintain an average rejection rate of over 99 % for CR under four different conditions. It was illustrated that the membranes had favorable operation stability and acid-base resistance properties.

Under neutral conditions, the water permeabilities of the M3 and PA3 membranes were comparable and did not change significantly after 24 h continuous operation, with average water permeabilities of approximately 5.41 ± 0.25 and 5.49 ± 0.21 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively. Compared to the pure water permeability of the M3 and PA3 membranes, the decrease in water permeability may be due to the presence of NaCl in the mixed solution, which increases the solubility of CR in the aqueous solution due to the “salt-in” effect, leading to an increase in the contamination of CR on the membrane surface and a decrease in the water permeability of the membrane. However, at the same time, due to the strong electronegativity of the membrane surface (Fig. S14) and the small average pore size (Fig. 9b), a high rejection rate of CR by the membranes was achieved. The water permeability of the M3 membrane was enhanced to 6.68 ± 0.23 and 8.06 ± 0.62 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 under acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively. It may be attributed to the small swelling phenomenon that occurred for the relatively loose selective layer on the membrane surface under acidic or alkaline conditions, which reduced the water passage resistance and subsequently enhanced the water permeability of the membrane.

The water permeability of the M4 membrane was only slightly increased (∼ 6.69 %) under acidic conditions compared to that under neutral conditions. Under alkaline conditions, the water permeability of the M4 membrane increased to 4.81 ± 0.82 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. This result might arise from the connection of the hydrophilic MP intermediate layer between the surface selective layer and the substrate support layer of the M4 membrane, endowing the M4 composite membrane with relatively strong structural stability. Resultantly, the membrane is able to resist the erosion of the strong acid and alkali environments to a certain extent and maintains a high retention rate (> 99 %) for CR. In addition, the membrane was hydraulic flushed once every 8 h in the continuous operation process, effectively slowing down the accumulation of pollutants on the membrane surface, which is also one of the reasons that the membrane can maintain a relatively stable water permeability during operation.

With the prolongation of operation time, the rejection rate of NaCl by the composite membranes decreased dramatically after filtration for 2 h and then tended to be stabilized. After 24 h of continuous operation, the rejection rate of NaCl by the M4 membrane was 31.23–35.09 % under four different environmental conditions (Fig. S17). The rejections of NaCl by the M3 and PA3 membranes under alkaline ambient conditions were higher than those in neutral conditions, which were about 27.43 % and 31.01 %, respectively (Fig. S17). It is mainly due to the enhancement in the negative charge density on the membrane surface of the M3 and PA3 membranes under alkaline conditions (Fig. S14), which favors the rejection of NaCl by the composite membranes due to the Donnan effect. After 24 h of operation under simulated sewage environmental conditions, the M3 and M4 composite membranes were still able to maintain a high rejection rate of CR and a higher permeability to NaCl. The selective separation factors of dye to salt calculated by eq. (3) were 104 and 115, respectively, which indicated that the M3 and M4 composite membranes were able to separate the CR/NaCl mixed solution efficiently. Besides, the separation factor of the M4 membrane was higher than that of the PA3 membrane (107). Resultantly, the N-GQDs composite membranes (M3 and M4 membranes) have good operating stability, acid and alkali resistance, and efficient dye/salt selective separation performance, which is expected to be used for the separation of dye/salt mixed pollutants in actual textile wastewater and water purification treatment.

The practical textile wastewater was taken from a denim factory in Guangzhou, and its physicochemical properties are shown in Table S5. The pretreatment of the raw textile industrial wastewater was carried out with a PVDF microfiltration membrane (0.45 μm). Fig. S18 exhibited the purification performance of the M3, M4, and PA3 membranes toward textile industrial wastewater. The membranes displayed a significant decline in water permeability (<1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) when filtration of the actual textile wastewater compared to pure water. It might be due to the existence of some complex surfactants, auxiliaries, and different dyes, increasing the mass transfer resistance and causing the fouling of the membranes [61,62]. Thus, the water permeability of the membranes was greatly decreased. Surprisingly, the decolorization of the actual textile wastewater by the membranes was excellent, and it could be observed that the color of the wastewater changed from deep blue to colorless after the filtration of the membranes. The decolorization efficiency reached >99 % considering the change in absorbance at the absorption peak of 672 nm (Fig. S18b). These results indicated that the as-prepared N-GQDs membrane can be used as a prospective candidate for the application of advanced treatment for practical textile wastewater.

In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method, and novel composite nanofiltration membranes were successfully fabricated by interfacial polymerization on PVDF-based membrane modified by multi-walled carbon nanotubes and poly(vinyl alcohol) and were used for treating dye wastewater. The thickness and pore size of the membranes can be flexibly designed and adjusted by regulating the additional amount of N-GQDs. The pure water permeability of the preferred M3 membrane was about 9.75 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and the rejection rate of small molecule dye MB could reach 97.24 %. Compared to our previous study, it achieved a significant elevation in MB rejection (∼ 37.79 %) and pure water permeability (∼ 78 %). The M4 membrane prepared with a higher content of N-GQDs (0.35 wt%) was able to achieve higher than 96 % rejection of charged dye molecules, even for the small molecule dyes MB (98.93 %) and MO (96.66 %), which was higher than the M3 membrane and our previous research work (notably enhanced by ∼39.48 % for MB). Compared to the piperazine polyamide (PA3) membrane, the M4 membrane possessed a smaller average pore size (∼ 0.46 nm), lower surface roughness, strong hydrophilicity and electronegativity (−43.33 mV). Based on these properties of the M4 membrane and the separation mechanisms of size sieving (steric hindrance) and charge effects, the M4 membrane still achieved a high selective separation performance (> 99 %) for CR after continuous operation in a mixed CR/NaCl solution for 24 h. In addition, the average permeance recovery rate of M4 membranes could reach >97 % in the two-cycle pollution experiments. A high rejection rate of CR was maintained without reducing the water permeability (compared with the neutral conditions) when continuous cross-flow filtered for 24 h in acidic, alkaline, or simulated wastewater environmental conditions. These results indicated that the M4 membranes possessed favorable antifouling features and acid-alkali resistance. Additionally, it's worth noting that the M3 and M4 membranes exhibited superior decolorization efficiency for real textile wastewater, indicating the potential to be used for the application of advanced treatment for actual textile wastewater and purification. Moreover, the flexible adjustment of the layer thickness and pore size of the membranes might provide an alternative for membrane integrated design and the realization of hierarchical separation or specific rejection toward the target pollutants.

Juan Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ji Lai Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Si Qun Tang: Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Huai Yang Zhou: Methodology, Investigation. Liang Xiu Tang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jun Zhao: Supervision.
